planting.note: Plant corms in early autumn for early spring flowers.

directions: Set the small corms about two inches deep in sharply drained, gritty soil in early autumn. This spring-flowering species opens starry white flowers striped purple on the outside as the snow retreats. It grows best in an alpine house or a very well-drained rockery.

planting.note: Plant corms in early autumn for late winter to early spring blooms.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in early autumn. This unusual species is one of the only yellow-flowered colchicums, blooming in late winter or early spring. It needs sharp drainage and a warm, dry summer dormancy, and does well in a bulb frame.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for early, heavily patterned flowers.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This early cultivar bears richly chequered purple-and-white flowers, showing its tessellated parentage. Give it a warm, sunny site and let the spring foliage fade before removing it.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for deeply colored autumn flowers.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in free-draining soil in late summer. Deep violet flowers with a distinct chequered pattern and a white throat open early in autumn. Site it in full sun and let the leaves die down naturally in early summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for richly colored, patterned blooms.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This cultivar produces deep rosy-purple flowers with a faint chequering and a pale center in autumn. Allow the spring leaves to mature fully before they yellow and are removed.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for large, scented autumn flowers.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil in late summer. Large pink flowers with a white throat, often lightly fragrant, open in autumn. Give the substantial spring foliage room and let it fade before tidying.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for strongly patterned autumn flowers.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This variety bears rosy-purple flowers with a clear chequered pattern in autumn. Leave the spring leaves in place until they yellow so the corm can rebuild.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for large, richly colored flowers.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil in late summer. Large deep purple-pink goblets open in autumn on strong stems. Allow the broad spring foliage to die down naturally before removal.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for a free-flowering early display.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This vigorous, popular cultivar produces many clear pink flowers early in autumn. Let the spring leaves feed the corm and remove them only after they wither.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for large tessellated flowers.

directions: Plant corms about four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil in late summer. Large violet-pink flowers with a noticeable chequered pattern open in autumn. Give the substantial spring leaves space and let them fade before tidying.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for deeply colored, patterned flowers.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This cultivar offers rich reddish-purple flowers with a strong chequered pattern and pale throat in autumn. Allow the spring foliage to die back naturally before removing it.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for star-shaped autumn flowers.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in free-draining soil in late summer. Slender-petaled pink flowers with a starry outline open in autumn. Let the spring leaves mature and yellow before they are cleared away.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for boldly patterned flowers.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This selection shows a striking chequerboard of purple and white on its autumn goblets. Give it a sunny site and let the spring foliage fade naturally before tidying.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for deeply colored autumn goblets.

directions: Plant the large corms about four inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in late summer. This selection bears unusually deep reddish-purple goblets in autumn on sturdy stems. Broad spring leaves follow and should be left to yellow before removal.

planting.note: Plant the large corms in late summer for big, reliable autumn flowers.

directions: Set the large corms about four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil in late summer. Big lilac-mauve goblets with white throats open dependably in autumn. Give the broad spring leaves room and let them die back naturally.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer to naturalize in grass or borders.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This selection produces soft rosy-pink chalices in autumn ahead of the foliage. It naturalizes well in short grass; delay mowing in spring until the leaves have died back.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer for an early, generous flowering.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in well-drained soil in late summer. This cultivar opens rosy-lilac flowers early in the autumn season. Let the spring foliage grow and fade naturally so the corm can build strength for next year.

planting.note: Set dormant tubers in late summer as the first pink flowers push up ahead of the leaves.

directions: Plant tubers about one inch deep with the rounded side down under the light shade of deciduous trees or shrubs. Flowers appear in autumn before the marbled ivy-shaped leaves unfurl, and the plant goes dormant through summer. Leave clumps undisturbed to self-sow and naturalize into broad sheets over the years.

planting.note: Plant tubers in late summer for pure white autumn bloom in dry shade.

directions: Set tubers an inch deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil beneath trees where summer rain is limited. Crisp white swept-back petals open in fall above patterned leaves. Top-dress with leaf mold each autumn and allow the colony to seed itself around.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for pink flowers over strikingly silvered foliage.

directions: Plant tubers shallowly in gritty, leaf-rich soil in dappled shade. This selection carries broad pewter-silver leaves veined and edged in green, adding winter interest long after the autumn pink flowers fade. Keep tubers on the dry side during their summer rest.

planting.note: Plant tubers in late summer for near-solid silver leaves under light shade.

directions: Set the tuber just below the surface in free-draining soil enriched with leaf mold. Leaves emerge almost entirely silver with a thin green rim, glowing in a shaded bed all winter. Pink autumn flowers rise before the foliage, and the plant rests dormant in summer.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for white flowers above silvered foliage.

directions: Plant tubers about an inch deep in humus-rich, well-drained shade. This form pairs pure white autumn flowers with heavily silvered leaves for a cool, luminous effect. Mulch with leaf mold and leave undisturbed to bulk up.

planting.note: Plant tubers in late summer for deep carmine autumn flowers.

directions: Set tubers shallowly in gritty soil under deciduous cover with good drainage. Rich ruby-magenta flowers open in fall ahead of the patterned foliage, deeper in color than the typical pink. Allow it to settle in and self-seed for a spreading display.

planting.note: Plant tubers in autumn for magenta-pink flowers in the depths of winter.

directions: Plant tubers an inch deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil in dappled winter light beneath deciduous trees. Rounded kidney-shaped leaves appear in fall, followed by stubby magenta, pink, or white flowers from midwinter into early spring. Leave colonies to naturalize and seed among snowdrops and hellebores.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for white winter flowers with a dark carmine nose.

directions: Set tubers just below the soil in a sheltered, free-draining spot in light shade. White flowers each marked with a small crimson blotch at the mouth open through the coldest months. Mulch lightly with leaf mold and let seedlings fill in around the parent.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for white flowers above silvered, green-edged leaves.

directions: Plant tubers shallowly in gritty, leaf-rich soil in a bright, sheltered corner. This selection combines pewter leaves with a fine green margin and white winter flowers touched with pink at the nose. Keep the crown from sitting wet and let it seed gently around.

planting.note: Plant tubers in autumn for pale, two-tone pink winter flowers.

directions: Set the tuber an inch deep in humus-rich, well-drained shade sheltered from harsh wind. Soft pink flowers carry a deeper rose zone toward the mouth for a gentle bicolor effect through winter. Underplant with early bulbs and allow it to naturalize slowly.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for winter flowers over broad silver leaves.

directions: Plant tubers just under the surface in free-draining, leaf-rich soil in dappled light. Seedlings of this group reliably produce rounded pewter-silver leaves with a narrow dark rim, topped by pink or occasionally white flowers in winter. A tough, showy foliage plant for the front of a shady bed.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for bright pink flowers above patterned silver leaves.

directions: Set tubers shallowly in gritty, humus-rich soil in a sheltered shaded position. This Ashwood selection offers strong pink flowers over well-marked silvery foliage through the winter months. Protect from waterlogging and let it self-sow to build a colony.

planting.note: Plant in spring for fragrant white summer flowers in cool shade.

directions: Set tubers shallowly in cool, limy, well-drained soil in dappled woodland light. This white form carries the same rich scent as the type, flowering from summer into autumn on a nearly evergreen plant. Avoid letting the tuber dry out completely and top-dress with leaf mold.

planting.note: Plant tubers in late summer for dainty scented pink flowers in autumn.

directions: Plant tubers an inch deep in gritty, free-draining soil in a warm, sheltered spot with light shade. Small twisted pale-pink flowers with a crimson mouth appear in fall alongside neatly patterned rounded leaves. It appreciates a dry summer rest and does well in a raised bed or alpine setting.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for autumn flowers over scalloped, pink-flushed new leaves.

directions: Set tubers shallowly in gritty, well-drained soil in a sheltered, lightly shaded position. Toothed leaves emerge flushed rose-pink on the upper surface before greening, and fringed pale pink flowers open in fall. Give it a warm, dry summer dormancy and sharp drainage to thrive.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for large, fragrant magenta flowers in early spring.

directions: Set tubers shallowly in humus-rich, well-drained soil in a sheltered, lightly shaded position. It bears some of the largest and most fragrant flowers in the genus, in deep magenta with a purple-and-white eye, over handsomely marbled leaves. Protect the early spring bloom from hard frost, ideally in a cold frame or wall-sheltered bed.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for large soft-pink spring flowers; best with frost protection.

directions: Plant tubers in gritty, free-draining soil in a protected spot or, in most gardens, an alpine house or cold frame. Broad pale-pink flowers with deeper markings and a distinctive musty scent open in early spring over marbled foliage. Keep frost off the buds and give a drier, warm summer rest.

planting.note: Plant tubers in autumn for scented flowers with flat, propeller-like petals in late winter.

directions: Set tubers shallowly in gritty, humus-rich soil in a sheltered, lightly shaded position with good drainage. Its sweetly scented pink flowers hold their petals out flat like tiny propellers, a distinctive trait, opening in late winter to early spring. Provide a warm, drier summer rest for the best flowering.

planting.note: Plant tubers in late summer for tiny veined white-to-pink flowers in autumn.

directions: Plant this smallest cyclamen just below the surface in gritty, sharply drained soil, ideally in a trough or alpine bed. Delicate white or pale pink flowers with fine grey veining open in fall over small rounded leaves. Its diminutive size suits container culture where it can be given a dry summer rest.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for miniature winter flowers; one of the hardiest species.

directions: Plant tubers shallowly in cool, humus-rich, well-drained soil in a sheltered alpine bed or trough. This high-altitude Turkish species is among the hardiest cyclamen, bearing tiny pink flowers close to plain green leaves in winter. It prefers cool, moist conditions rather than a hot summer bake.

planting.note: Plant in spring for strongly scented deep-pink flowers in late summer.

directions: Plant tubers in cool, gritty, humus-rich soil in a lightly shaded, rocky position with good drainage. Thick-textured leathery leaves accompany intensely fragrant deep pink flowers in late summer and early autumn. Keep it evenly cool and moist rather than baked, reflecting its Caucasian cliff origins.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for fragrant white autumn-into-winter flowers.

directions: Plant tubers in gritty, free-draining soil in a warm, sheltered spot or, in cold gardens, an alpine house. Sweetly scented white flowers with crimson-blotched noses and twisted petals open from autumn into winter over patterned leaves. Protect from hard frost and give a warm, drier summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for slender fragrant white spring flowers; needs frost protection.

directions: Plant tubers in sharply drained, humus-rich soil in a sheltered, frost-free position or an alpine house. Delicate, faintly fragrant white flowers, sometimes flushed pink, appear in spring over softly marbled leaves. Keep frost off the emerging growth and allow a warm summer rest.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for small fragrant veined white flowers in spring.

directions: Plant tubers shallowly in gritty, well-drained soil in a warm, protected niche or under glass. Small white flowers finely veined in pink and lightly scented open in spring above grey-green marbled leaves. It resents winter wet and hard frost, so a raised bed or pot suits it best.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for autumn flowers with protruding cones of anthers; grow under glass in cold climates.

directions: Plant the knobbly tuber in very free-draining, gritty soil in a warm greenhouse or frost-free frame. This distinctive Libyan species bears pink autumn flowers with a prominent projecting cone of anthers and boldly lobed leaves. It needs a hot, dry summer bake and complete protection from frost to succeed.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for slender, long-petalled deep-pink winter flowers.

directions: Plant tubers in gritty, humus-rich, well-drained soil in a sheltered, lightly shaded spot or under glass. Elegant deep pink flowers with notably long, narrow, strongly reflexed petals open in winter over marbled leaves. Related to Cyclamen coum but more tender, it benefits from frost protection in colder gardens.

planting.note: Start tubers indoors in shallow trays several weeks before the last frost, then move outdoors once nights stay mild.

directions: Press the dormant tuber hollow-side up into moist potting mix, barely covering it, and keep it warm and bright until sprouts appear. Grow in dappled shade in rich, well-drained soil and water evenly without wetting the crown. Blooms from midsummer to first frost; lift and dry the tubers before winter in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost in a sheltered, bright spot.

directions: Grow this cascading selection in baskets or tall pots of free-draining compost in dappled light. Keep evenly moist and feed regularly for months of flower. Soft pink pendant blooms trail over the container edge from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Start tubers under cover and plant out once frost has passed.

directions: Grow this graceful trailer in a basket or shady bed of loose, moist soil in filtered light. It forms bulbils in the leaf joints that can be gathered and grown on for new plants. Airy sprays of small orange flowers appear from midsummer through autumn.

planting.note: Grow warm and set out only after nights are reliably mild.

directions: Plant the tuber shallowly in rich, well-drained mix and keep it warm in bright shade. This Andean species has velvety dark leaves with pale veins and is one of the ancestors of modern garden begonias. Small yellow flowers rise above the foliage through summer.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a cool, bright, sheltered spot.

directions: Set the tuber just below the surface in gritty, humus-rich soil and give it cool, filtered light. A high-altitude Andean species and a key parent of tuberous hybrids, it dislikes heat and heavy wet. Bright orange-red flowers open on short stems in summer.

planting.note: Transplant compact plants out after all frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors months ahead and keep it warm and lit, or start from a tuber for quicker results. Grow compact plants in beds or pots of rich, well-drained soil in bright shade. Fully double scarlet flowers cover the mound nonstop from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Set out after frost into shaded beds or containers.

directions: Start seed warm under light or plant a tuber hollow-side up in moist mix. Keep plants in dappled shade with even moisture and steady, gentle feeding. Double golden-yellow blooms carry from early summer until the first frost.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a bright, shaded location.

directions: Grow from warm-sown seed or a dormant tuber set just at the soil surface. Provide filtered light, rich free-draining soil, and consistent moisture without soaking the crown. Clean double white flowers light up shade all season long.

planting.note: Plant out compact clumps once frost has passed.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of warm, moist mix, or plant a sprouting tuber shallowly. Set the compact plants in shaded beds or pots and keep the soil evenly damp. Double tangerine-orange flowers bloom continuously from summer to frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into filtered light.

directions: Start warm from seed under light or from a shallow-set tuber in moist mix. Grow in bright shade with rich, well-drained soil and steady watering. Double cream blooms edged in rose picotee appear from early summer through frost.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into shade or morning sun.

directions: Sow seed warm or start a tuber, then grow on in bright, filtered light. The dark bronze foliage tolerates a little more sun than green-leaved types if kept moist. Double yellow flowers stand out against the chocolate leaves from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant out into baskets after the last frost.

directions: Start seed warm under light or plant a tuber and grow on for a cascading basket plant. Site in bright shade and keep the free-draining mix evenly moist through summer. Large double rose-pink flowers trail over the edges from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Set out into hanging baskets once frost is past.

directions: Grow from warm-sown seed or a shallow-planted tuber for a trailing habit. Keep in filtered light with rich, moist, well-drained compost and feed regularly. Soft apricot double flowers cascade from baskets all summer until frost.

planting.note: Transplant to baskets after all frost has passed.

directions: Start warm from seed or a tuber and grow on until roots fill the pot. Plant in bright shade in a container of loose, moisture-retentive mix. Double white blooms tumble over the basket rim from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in bright, filtered light.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed warm and keep it lit and moist to germinate. Grow the plants in beds or pots of rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade. Single white flowers brushed with a red-orange picotee edge open from summer to frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into a shaded spot.

directions: Start seed warm under light or grow on a young plant in bright shade. Use rich, free-draining soil and water evenly, avoiding the crown. Single blooms of white edged in rose-pink appear continuously through summer.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into shaded beds or pots.

directions: Sow the tiny seed on the surface of warm, moist mix under bright light. Grow the upright plants in filtered light with steady moisture and feeding. Large flowers in blended apricot, rose, and gold tones with a picotee edge face upward all summer.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into bright shade.

directions: Start warm from seed or plant a tuber shallowly in moist mix. This early, free-flowering strain suits pots and shaded borders in rich, well-drained soil. Large double flowers in warm colors open weeks ahead of many tuberous types.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed warm and keep it lit until it sprouts. Grow on and plant out after frost in rich, moist, free-draining soil in filtered light. Large double flowers blend soft apricot and orange shades through summer.

planting.note: Set out into baskets once frost is past.

directions: Plant a tuber hollow-side up near the surface in loose, moisture-retentive mix. This pendulous strain is bred for hanging baskets in bright shade. Semi-double flowers in a range of colors trail and bloom from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Start tubers indoors and plant out after frost.

directions: Sprout the large tuber hollow-side up in shallow, moist mix before setting it out. Grow in bright shade in rich soil that never waterlogs. Full double flowers carry a contrasting colored margin, or picotee edge, from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start tubers indoors weeks before setting them out after frost.

directions: Begin the tuber in a warm, bright spot, hollow-side up, just covered with damp mix. Move to rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade after frost and stake tall stems. The big double flowers have a high, rosebud-like center and bloom all summer.

planting.note: Sprout tubers under cover, then plant out after frost.

directions: Press the tuber hollow-side up into moist mix and keep it warm until shoots form. Plant in bright shade in rich, free-draining soil and water evenly at the base. The fringed, carnation-like double flowers open from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Start tubers early indoors and set out once frost has passed.

directions: Sprout the tuber in shallow trays of damp mix, hollow-side up, before planting out. Grow in filtered light in humus-rich, well-drained soil and support the heavy stems. Large, fully double blooms shaped like camellias appear all summer.

planting.note: Begin tubers indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Start the tuber hollow-side up in moist mix in a warm, bright place. Plant into rich, free-draining soil in bright shade and keep the moisture steady. Double flowers show a fine, thin band of contrasting color along the petal edge through summer.

planting.note: Plant into baskets after the last frost.

directions: Start warm from seed under light or from a tuber for a cascading basket plant. Grow in bright shade with rich, moisture-retentive, free-draining mix and regular feeding. Golden-yellow double flowers edged in orange trail over the pot from summer to frost.

planting.note: Set rhizome divisions out in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant rhizomes just below the surface in well-drained soil, spacing clumps to allow the tall fans of foliage room to arch. Water to establish, then rely on this tough plant to tolerate drought and neglect once rooted. Large white flowers marked with yellow and violet open in flushes about every two weeks through the warm season; leave spent stems standing since they rebloom.

planting.note: Divide and replant clumps in spring for the fastest establishment.

directions: Set divisions shallowly in average, well-drained soil where they can form a soft grassy clump. Cut old flowering stems back only after several seasons, since new buds form on the same stalks. Pale yellow flowers with three dark maroon blotches appear in repeated flushes from late spring into autumn.

planting.note: Establish in frost-free gardens in spring or early summer.

directions: Give this large island species rich, free-draining soil and shelter from hard frost, as it is the most tender of the group. Water regularly through the growing season to support its broad fans of foliage. Big pure-white flowers rise above the leaves in summer; divide congested clumps only when necessary.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: This forest species prefers dappled shade and soil that stays evenly moist and humus-rich. Its wide, glossy leaves are the ornamental highlight, so protect them from scorching sun and cold wind. Short-lived cream flowers appear among the foliage in the warmer months.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant the shallow rhizomes in well-drained ground and water sparingly once established. This summer-rainfall species tolerates light frost better than the tender forest kinds. Soft yellow flowers with darker markings open on branched stems in spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this hybrid is grown from division only.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface in a sunny, well-drained bed and keep newly planted clumps watered until rooted. As a sterile-tending hybrid it is propagated by division rather than seed. Creamy flowers accented with warm apricot-orange centers open in repeated flushes through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn; give a dry summer rest.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in sandy, sharply drained mix and keep just moist during the cool growing months. This rare species does especially well in pots where its summer dormancy can be kept bone dry. Large white flowers each bearing a brilliant blue-and-black eye open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in late summer to autumn for a long autumn-to-spring display.

directions: Plant corms a couple of inches deep in well-drained soil where they can naturalize into drifts. This vigorous species flowers over an unusually long season and tolerates more neglect than most of its relatives. Clear violet-blue flowers with yellow nectar guides open on tall branched stems from autumn into spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in a lean, gritty medium and water only during the cool growing period. The slender single leaf and wiry stems suit rock gardens and containers. Delicate blue to lilac flowers dominated by three large outer petals appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn and keep dry in summer.

directions: Grow corms in deep, sharply drained sand two to three inches down and water steadily through winter. A dry, warm summer dormancy is essential to prevent the corms rotting. The largest-flowered peacock moraea, it opens broad violet blooms with a shimmering blue-green central eye in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for spring flowers.

directions: Set corms about two inches deep in a fast-draining, sandy mix and water through the cool season only. Keep container-grown plants completely dry once the leaves die back for summer. Brilliant orange-red flowers centered with a dark iridescent eye open on sunny spring days.

planting.note: Plant the small corms in autumn near the soil surface.

directions: Plant these dwarf corms shallowly in gritty soil and grow them in pots where their tiny stature can be appreciated up close. Water through the cool growing months and dry off fully for summer. Low, near-stemless flowers in white, blue, or yellow with fringed leaf edges open early in the season.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in full sun.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in well-drained sandy soil and water through winter and early spring. The blooms are fleeting, each lasting a day, but are pleasantly fragrant. Flowers range from white to lilac or blue with yellow centers and appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn; strictly dry in summer.

directions: This critically rare species is best grown in pots of sharp sand so its summer rest can be kept perfectly dry. Plant corms two inches deep and water only during winter growth. Striking dark violet flowers with a black-bearded eye and reflexed petals open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in fast-draining soil.

directions: Set corms about two inches deep in gritty soil and water through the cool growing season only. A completely dry summer dormancy keeps the corms sound. Vivid orange flowers with a metallic blue-green central eye open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn; hardier than the Cape species.

directions: Plant these small Mediterranean corms two inches deep in lean, well-drained soil where they naturalize freely. They tolerate more cold than the South African species and appreciate a warm, dry summer. Violet-blue flowers with a yellow blotch open in the afternoon during spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring; this summer-rainfall species grows in the warm months.

directions: Unlike the winter growers, this species grows through summer and needs regular moisture then, tolerating winter cold better. Plant corms a few inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil beside a pond or damp border. Fragrant bright yellow flowers open on tall stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for summer growth.

directions: This robust grassland species keeps a single long, tough leaf and forms sturdy clumps over time. Plant corms three inches deep in well-drained soil and water through the growing season. Large golden-yellow flowers rise on tall stems over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring; among the hardiest species.

directions: A high-altitude summer grower, this species withstands more frost than most and grows through the warm season. Plant corms three inches deep in moist but well-drained ground and water in summer. Cream to pale yellow flowers flushed with green appear on tall stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in a sunny site.

directions: Plant the small corms two inches deep in well-drained sandy soil and water through the cool months. Give a warm, dry rest in summer to keep the corms healthy. Modest flowers in muted brown, greenish, or dull violet shades open on wiry stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for spring bloom.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in gritty, fast-draining soil and water only during winter growth. This species is prized by collectors for its unusual color range rather than showiness. Curiously scented flowers in blends of brown, maroon, yellow, and dusky purple open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in full sun.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in sandy, well-drained soil and water through the cool growing season. Give a dry summer dormancy once the foliage yellows. Tall stems carry clusters of bright yellow flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn near seeps or moist ground.

directions: This species forms tall, much-branched stems and enjoys damp ground during its winter growth. Plant corms two inches deep in well-drained but moisture-retentive soil. Airy sprays of bright yellow flowers appear in spring on the widely branching stems.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in gritty soil.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in sharp, sandy soil and water through the cool months only. Keep the summer rest warm and dry to protect the dormant corms. Cheerful orange or cream flowers marked with a dark eye open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for summer flowering.

directions: A summer-growing species, it needs moisture through the warm months and tolerates winter cold when dormant. Plant corms two to three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Soft apricot to cream flowers with an orange center open over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: This low species favors damp ground during winter growth, so keep it evenly moist then. Plant corms two inches deep in gritty soil and dry off for summer. Bright yellow flowers with a slender-clawed shape open close to the ground in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant the small corms two inches deep in sandy soil and water only through the cool growing months. This widespread species is variable and easy for beginners. Small flowers in cream, yellow, or dull violet, often greenish outside, open on branched stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for summer growth.

directions: A summer-rainfall grassland species, it grows and flowers during the warm months and rests in winter. Plant corms two inches deep in well-drained soil and water through the growing season. Pale primrose to cream flowers open low among the grasses in spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring in moist ground.

directions: This summer grower thrives in seasonally damp grassland and needs moisture through the warm months. Plant corms two to three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Yellow flowers open on branched stems in summer above narrow foliage.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in sandy soil and water through the cool growing period only. Give the dormant corms a warm, dry summer rest. Variable flowers in soft salmon, yellow, cream, or brownish tones open on slender stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring for warm-season growth.

directions: A summer-growing highveld species, it needs moisture in the warm months and tolerates cold winters when dormant. Plant corms two inches deep in well-drained soil. Yellow to greenish flowers with a nodding habit open in summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in sandy soil.

directions: Plant the small corms two inches deep in fast-draining soil and keep just moist through winter. A dry summer dormancy suits the resting corms. Charming flowers in salmon, pink, yellow, or cream with contrasting nectar guides open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn; excellent in containers.

directions: This dwarf dry-country species has distinctive coiled leaves and suits sharply drained pots. Plant corms two inches deep in gritty soil and water sparingly during cool growth. Fragrant white to pale lilac flowers with darker markings open low to the ground in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the small corms two inches deep in sandy, sharply drained soil and water through the cool season only. It is well suited to pot culture where its low blooms can be seen. Dusky violet to blackish flowers with a paler center open near the ground in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for a colorful spring show.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in fast-draining sandy soil and water through winter growth only. Keep the summer dormancy warm and dry. Cheerful flowers in a wide color range from orange and salmon to pink, yellow, and blue open in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn in full sun.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in well-drained sandy soil and water through the cool growing months. Give a dry, warm rest through summer. White flowers with pointed petals and dark central markings open on branched stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in warm spring soil after frost danger passes.

directions: Push corms three inches into loose, gritty soil and space them a hand's width apart in full sun. The outer petals open clean white while the central cup stays speckled maroon and gold. Give even moisture during growth; blooms appear in mid to late summer with each flower lasting only a day, so plant a generous group for a steady show.

planting.note: Set corms out once nights stay reliably above frost.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in sharp-draining soil and full sun after the ground warms. This form carries golden-yellow outer petals over a spotted throat. Keep the bed moist but never soggy; expect the first flowers roughly ten to twelve weeks after planting, with fresh blooms opening daily through the warm weeks.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost into warm, gritty soil.

directions: Set corms about three inches deep and four inches apart in a sunny, well-drained bed. The petals open a soft pale cream to primrose over a darker patterned center. Provide steady summer water and lift the corms in autumn where winters freeze; blooms open one at a time across midsummer.

planting.note: Wait until frost has passed and the soil is warm to plant.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep, pointed tip upward, in full sun and loose soil. Flowers open in soft rose-lilac tones with a spotted heart. Water regularly through summer and mulch lightly to hold moisture; the single-day blooms come in succession from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set out corms after frost, spacing for a bold summer clump.

directions: Bury corms three to four inches deep in gritty, sun-baked soil after the last frost. The broad outer petals glow deep scarlet-red above a boldly spotted center. Keep evenly watered while growing; flowering runs through the hottest weeks, each bloom lasting a day and quickly followed by the next.

planting.note: Plant into warm soil once spring frosts are finished.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep and four apart in full sun with excellent drainage. This form opens vivid orange to flame-scarlet petals over a patterned throat. Water steadily and feed lightly in midsummer; the daily flowers keep coming for weeks, so mass the corms for a continuous display.

planting.note: Plant corms after the frost-free date in warm ground.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in sharply drained soil under full sun. Petals open a rich carmine-pink with a spotted golden center. Keep the soil evenly moist through the growing months and lift corms before freezing weather; blooms appear singly across high summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring after the soil warms and frost has cleared.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. This Mexican species carries smaller nodding flowers heavily checkered in maroon and greenish-yellow. Water moderately during growth and keep it on the dry side once foliage dies back; expect intriguing blooms in the second half of summer.

planting.note: Set corms out in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in sharply drained soil with full sun. The dainty nodding bells are dusky pink to maroon and finely checkered like a fritillary. Give moderate water while in leaf and a drier rest afterward; the delicate flowers appear through mid to late summer and reward a close look.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost into warm, free-draining ground.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in sandy, well-drained soil under full sun. This slender species produces several small starry flowers per stem in soft mauve to lilac shades. Water moderately in the growing season and let it dry between flushes; blooms open successively through summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring; this higher-elevation species tolerates cool nights.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in rich but well-drained soil in full sun. Once classed in Rigidella, this species holds small upright cups of glowing orange-red on tall wiry stems. Keep it evenly watered through summer; the vivid flowers rise above the foliage from midsummer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost; give this tall species room to stretch.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Formerly known as Rigidella flammea, it sends up tall stems carrying nodding scarlet flowers with reflexed petals. Water generously while growing and stake in exposed sites; flowering peaks in late summer.

planting.note: Plant corms into warm spring soil once frost risk ends.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in gritty soil and full sun. The broad three-petaled flowers resemble the common tiger flower with a boldly spotted center. Keep evenly moist during active growth and allow a dry dormancy; the day-long blooms appear in succession through the summer heat.

planting.note: Plant corms in warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in sharply drained, sandy soil in full sun. This compact Mexican species carries small pink to rose flowers marked with a spotted throat. Water moderately during growth and keep dry when dormant; blooms open one at a time in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in gritty soil with full sun and excellent drainage. This small species opens pale yellow to cream flowers with a lightly spotted center. Keep it modestly watered while in leaf and quite dry in dormancy; the delicate blooms come through summer.

planting.note: Set corms out once soil warms after the last frost.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in sandy, fast-draining soil in full sun. As the name suggests, its flowers are cleaner and less heavily spotted than the common tiger flower. Give moderate water in growth and a dry rest afterward; expect single-day blooms across the summer.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring after frost into warm ground.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This dwarf species carries small starry flowers in soft pink to lilac tones. Water sparingly but regularly while in leaf and keep dry in dormancy; the modest blooms suit rock gardens and containers and open through summer.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost in warm, sandy soil.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in sharply drained soil under full sun. This small Mexican species opens pale lilac to whitish flowers with a spotted center. Provide moderate summer moisture and a dry winter rest; the flowers appear a few at a time across the warm season.

planting.note: Set corms out into warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This species from the Durango highlands bears small yellow to orange flowers marked at the throat. Water moderately during growth and keep dry when dormant; blooms open in succession through midsummer.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring after frost into warm, dry soil.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in very free-draining, sandy soil in full sun. This drought-adapted Mexican species carries small yellow to buff flowers. Water lightly during active growth and keep it dry through its long dormancy; the flowers appear briefly in summer and suit a bulb frame or gravel garden.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost into warm, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun. This dwarf species from Oaxaca bears small flowers in warm yellow to orange shades. Keep watering light and let the corms dry fully in dormancy; the little blooms open through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant corms once the soil warms after the last frost.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. This Mexican species produces small flowers in soft yellow to cream tones with a spotted heart. Provide moderate moisture in growth and a dry dormancy; blooms open singly across the warm season.

planting.note: Plant corms in warm spring soil after frost danger ends.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This species carries small flowers in muted yellow and brown tones with fine spotting. Water moderately while in leaf and keep dry during dormancy; the subtle blooms appear across mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set corms out after frost into warm, free-draining ground.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun. This slender Mexican species opens small flowers in pale lilac to whitish shades marked at the center. Give even moisture during growth and a dry rest afterward; the flowers come a few at a time through summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers shallowly in spring in containers or a sheltered bed.

directions: Nestle the tubers about an inch under the mix and water them in. This selection carries especially dark burgundy-purple leaflets that fold down at night, so give it bright, filtered light to keep the pigment intense. Small pale-lilac blooms rise above the foliage through the warm months.

planting.note: Start tubers in spring; grows readily as a houseplant year-round.

directions: Set the pale tubers an inch deep in free-draining mix and keep lightly moist. The airy green triangular leaflets fold up each evening and open again by morning. Clusters of dainty white flowers appear over a long season; divide crowded clumps in early spring.

planting.note: Plant shallowly in spring in pots or as a border edging.

directions: Tuck the tubers just under the surface of a well-drained container mix and water gently. This selection has wine-purple foliage that stays compact and mounded, making it a tidy filler in mixed pots. Soft pink flowers dot the plant through the growing season; trim back tired leaves to freshen the clump.

planting.note: Set tubers just below the surface after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant the small tubers an inch deep in loose mix and keep evenly damp during active growth. The near-black velvety leaflets contrast beautifully with silver or chartreuse companions in containers. Pale pink blooms appear intermittently; a short dry rest will reset a plant that has flagged in midsummer heat.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in spring once soil has warmed.

directions: Set the little bulbs about two inches deep in well-drained soil or a pot, pointed end up. Each four-parted leaf carries a bold dark maroon blotch at its center, hence the name. Rosy-red flowers rise above the foliage in summer; lift and store the bulbs dry over winter where the ground freezes.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a warm, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the small bulbs two inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil. The clover-like leaves each have four leaflets and are considered a good-luck charm. Bright rose flowers appear through summer; the bulbs are frost-tender, so grow in containers and overwinter dry in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant the fibrous-coated corms in early spring in sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant the small brown corms about two inches deep in gritty, very well-drained soil, ideal for a raised bed or trough. The pleated, folded grey-green leaves form a low tuft. Cupped pink flowers with darker centers open in late spring and early summer; this Andean species tolerates real winter cold but hates wet, heavy soil.

planting.note: Plant the scaly rhizomes in early spring in a gritty alpine bed.

directions: Set the small scaly rhizomes an inch or so deep in sharply drained, humus-rich grit. The folded, glaucous fan-shaped leaves form a neat cushion prized by rock gardeners. Large white to pale-pink cup-shaped flowers appear in early summer; give it cool conditions and protect from summer baking.

planting.note: Plant the little rhizomes in early spring in a scree or trough.

directions: Plant the scaly rhizomes shallowly in gritty, free-draining soil that stays cool. This selection bears rich rose-pink flowers over the same folded, silvery cushion foliage. Blooms open in early summer; divide congested clumps in spring to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant the slender rhizomes in early spring in gritty alpine conditions.

directions: Set the small worm-like rhizomes just under the surface in sharply drained scree. The crinkled blue-green leaflets are finely toothed and hug the ground. Fragrant flowers in shades of steel-blue, violet, and pink with darker veining open in early summer; keep it lean and well-drained.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in a gritty raised bed or trough.

directions: Plant the small rhizomes shallowly in free-draining alpine soil. This garden hybrid of O. enneaphylla and O. laciniata forms a low cushion of pleated grey-green leaves. Veined lilac-blue flowers open in early summer; it thrives in the cool, sharp drainage of a rock garden or trough.

planting.note: Plant the tuber in autumn to begin its cool-season growth cycle.

directions: Set the tuber an inch or two deep in a very gritty, fast-draining mix in a shallow pot. Each stem unfurls a whorl of many tiny leaflets resembling a miniature palm frond, forming a striking geometric rosette. Small pale-pink flowers appear in the cool months; keep it nearly dry during its summer dormancy to avoid rot.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for winter and spring flowers.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about an inch deep in gritty, free-draining soil and water lightly to start growth. Broad rounded leaflets sit low, and large funnel-shaped flowers in rose-purple, pink, or white open wide in full sun. This Cape species grows through the cool season and rests dry in summer; ease off watering as the foliage dies down.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in pots or a warm, dry-summer bed.

directions: Set the bulbs about an inch deep in a sandy, well-drained mix. Large, leathery three-parted leaves support tall stems topped with showy rose-pink flowers, among the biggest in the genus. Bloom comes in autumn and early winter; keep the bulbs dry during their summer rest.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in autumn for winter bloom.

directions: Plant the tiny bulbs about an inch deep in a gritty pot mix. Low tufts of small hairy leaflets give rise to cheerful flowers in salmon, apricot, pink, or yellow, often with a contrasting throat. This variable Cape bulb blooms in the cool season and needs a dry summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn; growth trails as the season progresses.

directions: Set the small bulbs an inch deep in a fast-draining mix, ideal for a hanging pot. Wiry stems clothed in tiny narrow leaves lengthen and trail over the season. Rich rosy-purple flowers with a yellow eye smother the plant in late autumn and winter; keep dry through summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in gritty, free-draining soil.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about an inch deep in a very well-drained sandy mix. This species has unusual thick, finger-like grey-green leaflets held in a fan. Flowers range from yellow to white, pink, or salmon and open in the cool season; give it a dry summer rest.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for cool-season bloom.

directions: Set the tiny bulbs about an inch deep in gritty, sharply drained mix. Low mats of small leaflets carry an unusual warm orange flower rarely seen in the genus. Bloom comes in autumn and winter; withhold water as leaves fade and store the bulbs dry over summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn; contain it, as it self-multiplies freely.

directions: Plant the small bulbs a couple inches deep in any well-drained soil, ideally confined to a pot since this species spreads vigorously. Clover-like leaves, sometimes flecked with purple, form loose mounds. Bright buttercup-yellow flowers appear from late winter into spring; be aware it is invasive in mild climates and best grown as a contained container plant.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs in fall in a woodland-edge or prairie bed.

directions: Set the small scaly bulbs about two inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. This native North American species carries clover-like leaves that are often purple-tinged beneath. Clusters of rosy-violet flowers rise on leafless stalks in spring, sometimes reblooming in fall; it naturalizes gently in dappled shade.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring for late-spring to summer flowers.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about an inch deep in free-draining, humus-rich mix. Fresh green clover leaves form a low tuft. Vivid magenta-pink flowers with a white-and-yellow eye open in flushes from late spring; grow in pots and keep the bulbs frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring; foliage rests in summer and reappears to flower in fall.

directions: Set the small bulbs an inch deep in gritty, well-drained soil. This Chilean species sends up bright green leaves in spring that die back and then return with the flowers. Cheerful golden-yellow blooms open in autumn on a compact tuft; keep drier during its brief summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a gritty, well-drained pot.

directions: Plant the tiny bulbs about an inch deep in sharply drained mix. Small clover leaflets hug the ground in a neat low mat. Bright pink flowers with a yellow throat open in the cool months; give this Cape species a dry summer rest to keep the bulbs healthy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-autumn and winter bloom.

directions: Set the small bulbs about an inch deep in a very gritty, free-draining mix. This selection forms tight rosettes of silvery, densely hairy leaflets that are attractive even out of bloom. Large bright-yellow flowers open in autumn and early winter; keep it lean and give a dry summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant the woody tuberous rootstock in spring in a sunny border.

directions: Set the knobby tuberous rootstock just below the surface in well-drained garden soil. Fresh green clover leaves build a dense mound. Clusters of bright rose-pink flowers appear over a very long season from late spring into fall; deadhead lightly and divide crowded clumps in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a cool, moist, part-shaded spot.

directions: Set the thread-like rhizomes just under the surface in cool, humus-rich soil that never dries out. Tiny bronze-green clover leaves knit into a flat mat only an inch or two tall. This selection produces fully double, pure-white pompon flowers in late spring and summer; ideal as a shady groundcover or trough carpet.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-season bloom.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about an inch deep in a very well-drained, sandy pot mix. Slender wiry stems carry tiny narrow leaflets and a somewhat sprawling habit. Large bright rose-pink flowers open in autumn and early winter, glowing in full sun; keep dry during summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for winter-into-spring flowers.

directions: Set the small bulbs about an inch deep in gritty, free-draining mix. Clover-like leaves with slightly flattened stalks form a low clump. This double-flowered form bears rosette-like bright yellow blooms in the cool season; the bulb rests dry through summer, so taper off water as leaves yellow.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for cool-season display.

directions: Plant the tiny bulbs about an inch deep in a sharply drained sandy mix. Small clover leaflets, sometimes edged in fine hairs, form a low tuft. Bright lemon-yellow flowers open flat in the sun through autumn and winter; keep this Cape bulb dry during its summer rest.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a gritty, free-draining pot.

directions: Set the small bulbs about an inch deep in a very well-drained sandy mix. This Cape species has finely divided, needle-like leaflets that give it a soft, feathery look. Bright magenta-pink flowers with a paler eye open in the cool season; provide a dry summer dormancy to prevent rot.

planting.note: Set rhizomes just below the surface in spring in rich, constantly moist soil.

directions: Plant the fleshy rhizome about two inches deep in heavy, moisture-retentive soil that never fully dries; this species tolerates boggy pond margins. Keep it well watered through the growing season and mulch the crown where winters are marginal. Broad white spathes with a golden central spadix open from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A hardier white form; plant rhizomes in spring in moist, fertile ground.

directions: Bury the rhizome two inches deep in rich soil and give it steady moisture through summer. This selection withstands slightly colder winters than the type, so a deep mulch usually carries it through in borderline zones. Expect classic pure-white funnel-shaped spathes from late spring onward.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes shallowly in spring in consistently damp, fertile soil.

directions: Set the rhizome about two inches deep in humus-rich soil at a pond edge or moist border. Water generously and feed once in early summer for the strongest stems. The large spathes are streaked and flushed apple-green with a white throat, opening through summer and lasting well when cut.

planting.note: Give this towering white form space and permanently moist, rich ground.

directions: Plant the large rhizome two to three inches deep in deep, fertile soil that stays wet, ideally in a bog or streamside setting. Keep it constantly moist and shelter the tall stems from strong wind. Enormous white spathes on stems that can reach shoulder height appear across summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in moist, fertile soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Set the rhizome two inches deep in rich, damp soil and water freely all season. The white spathes carry a soft rose-pink flush and a plum stain deep in the throat. Blooms open from late spring and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: A compact white form good for containers and small moist borders.

directions: Plant the rhizome about two inches deep in a large pot or damp border and never let it dry out. Its smaller stature suits patio containers standing in a saucer of water. Slender white spathes appear from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost once the soil has warmed, in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the tuber three to four inches deep with the growth eyes facing up in fertile, free-draining soil. Unlike the white calla it dislikes waterlogging, so water steadily but let the surface dry between soakings. Bright golden-yellow spathes rise above spotted foliage in mid to late summer; lift tubers before frost in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the tuber three inches deep in free-draining, fertile soil and water moderately once shoots emerge. This is the parent of most modern pink hybrids and stays fairly compact. Narrow rose-pink spathes appear in summer; store the dormant tuber dry and frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set the tuber about three inches deep in rich, sharply drained soil and water moderately through the summer. This robust species carries broad rich-yellow spathes, often with a deep purple blotch at the base. Flowers appear in summer; keep the dormant tuber dry and frost-free overwinter.

planting.note: A winter-growing species; start rhizomes in autumn where mild.

directions: Plant the rhizome a couple of inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil and water during its autumn-to-spring growth period, then keep it drier when dormant in summer. It resembles the white calla but the white spathes carry a light, freesia-like scent. Flowers open in spring after the winter growth flush.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil or containers.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep, growth points up, in rich but free-draining soil and water moderately once growth starts. The compact spathes are white-edged with a deep violet-purple center, held above white-speckled leaves. Flowers open in summer; lift and store the tuber dry over winter in cold zones.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the tuber three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and avoid waterlogging. The deep purple-black spathes contrast strikingly with spotted green foliage. Bloom comes in summer; store the dried tuber frost-free through winter where it is not hardy.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in rich, sharply drained soil and water steadily without letting the ground stay soggy. The near-black, glossy purple spathes make a dramatic cut flower. Flowers appear in summer; lift the tuber before frost and store it dry.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil or pots.

directions: Set the tuber three to four inches deep, eyes up, in fertile free-draining soil and water moderately once it sprouts. Its name means Black Forest, describing the deep chocolate-black spathes above white-flecked leaves. Bloom is in summer; keep the dormant tuber dry and frost-free in winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and water moderately through the season. The spathes open bright yellow and mature to fiery orange and red, so a clump shows several shades at once. Flowers appear in summer; lift and dry the tuber for winter where frost occurs.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the tuber three to four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil and keep it evenly moist but never wet. The warm spathes blend mango-orange and gold with soft pink shading. Flowers open in summer; store the tuber dry and frost-free once foliage dies back.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep, growth eyes up, in rich, sharply drained soil and water moderately. The spathes glow a deep garnet-red and pair well with dark or silver foliage plants. Bloom comes through summer; lift and store the tuber dry before the first hard frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil or containers.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and water moderately once shoots appear. The soft peach-to-salmon spathes carry a warmer coral flush as they age. Flowers open in summer; keep the dormant tuber dry and frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the tuber three inches deep in rich, free-draining soil and water evenly through growth. The white spathes are edged and blushed with a delicate rose-pink that deepens as flowers mature. Bloom comes in summer; lift and store the tuber dry over winter in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three to four inches deep in fertile, sharply drained soil and water moderately, avoiding soggy conditions. This hybrid gives clean pure-white spathes on a compact plant, making it a tidy container calla. Flowers open in summer; store the dried tuber frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and water moderately once growth begins. The vivid orange-red spathes are among the boldest of the summer hybrids. Bloom comes in summer; lift the tuber before frost and keep it dry until spring.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the tuber three inches deep, eyes upward, in fertile, well-drained soil and water moderately through the season. The rich golden-yellow spathes rise above lightly spotted foliage. Flowers open in summer; store the dormant tuber dry and frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in rich, sharply drained soil and water moderately without waterlogging. The clear yellow spathes brighten a container or front border. Flowers appear in summer; lift and dry the tuber for frost-free winter storage.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the tuber three to four inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and water moderately once it sprouts. The unusual spathes blend bronze, chocolate-brown and dusky rose tones. Bloom comes in summer; store the dried tuber frost-free until spring in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in rich, free-draining soil and water moderately through the growing season. The spathes are a soft rose-pink deepening toward the throat, set off by dark speckled leaves. Flowers open in summer; lift and store the tuber dry over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the tuber three inches deep in fertile, sharply drained soil and water moderately once shoots appear. The deep golden-yellow spathes are held on sturdy compact stems that suit pots. Flowers open in summer; keep the dormant tuber dry and frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and water moderately once growth starts. This compact selection carries rich rose-pink spathes above lightly spotted leaves. Flowers open in summer and are excellent for cutting; store the tuber dry over winter where frosty.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the tuber three inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil and water moderately through the season. Its warm orange spathes are flushed with red and gold for a sunset effect. Compact and pot-friendly, it blooms in summer; lift and dry the tuber before frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep, eyes up, in rich, sharply drained soil and water moderately. This compact calla bears warm golden-yellow spathes on tidy stems. Flowers open in summer; keep the dormant tuber dry and frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the tuber three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and water moderately once shoots appear. The bright red spathes on this compact selection make a strong container display. Flowers open in summer; lift and store the tuber dry over winter where frost threatens.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep in rich, free-draining soil and water moderately through growth. The soft rose-pink spathes sit above compact, lightly speckled foliage. Flowers open in summer; keep the dried dormant tuber frost-free until spring.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the tuber three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil and water moderately once it sprouts. This compact calla bears creamy ivory-to-pale-yellow spathes that suit soft color schemes. Flowers open in summer; lift and store the tuber dry over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the tuber three inches deep, eyes up, in rich, sharply drained soil and water moderately. The deep purple-burgundy spathes give this compact selection a dark, dramatic look in containers. Flowers open in summer; keep the dormant tuber dry and frost-free through winter.

planting.note: Set bulbs out once soil is warm and all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant the large bulbs with their necks just at or slightly above the soil surface in rich, consistently moist ground. Keep the roots damp through the growing season, as this species naturally grows near coastal wetlands. Fragrant white flowers with long spidery segments open through summer above strap-shaped evergreen leaves.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in warm soil, or grow in a large pot to move indoors in cold zones.

directions: Plant bulbs shallowly in moisture-retentive soil and give them dappled shade in the hottest climates. The broad, glossy leaves make it a handsome container specimen even out of bloom. Large white flowers with a faint green throat appear in flushes and carry a strong evening fragrance.

planting.note: Plant outdoors once frost danger passes; lift and store dry over winter in cold zones.

directions: Set bulbs about four inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil after the ground warms. In zones colder than 8, dig the bulbs before frost, cure them, and keep them dry indoors for winter. Cupped white flowers with fringed, daffodil-like centers open within a couple months and are pleasantly fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in warm soil after frost; lift for dry winter storage where hardy.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep in loose, rich soil once nights stay mild. Feed lightly during active growth and keep the soil evenly moist for the best flowering. Pure white flowers with a spidery corona and reflexed segments open in early summer and carry a light scent.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm soil; store bulbs dry over winter in cold regions.

directions: Set bulbs several inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil after the last frost. Keep them watered through summer and allow the foliage to yellow naturally before lifting in autumn. Soft primrose-yellow flowers with green-striped throats open in early to mid summer and are lightly fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in warm spring soil; lift and dry-store where winters freeze.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in well-drained ground once the soil has warmed. Water steadily during growth and taper off as leaves fade at season's end. Clean white flowers with a delicate green-tinged cup appear in early summer and open with a sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring in damp, low ground.

directions: Plant bulbs in wet meadow soil or beside a pond with the neck near the surface. This Gulf Coast native tolerates seasonal flooding and prefers ground that never bakes dry. Fragrant white flowers with a golden eye rise in spring, earlier than most spider lilies.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a wet site or streamside bed with moving water if possible.

directions: This rare shoals lily naturally roots in rocky riverbeds, so give it constantly wet, well-oxygenated soil in full sun. It is challenging away from flowing water but rewards a bog or streamside planting. Showy white flowers with a broad frilled cup open in late spring and last only a day or two each.
